commits:
[{'sha': 'd8e2ed109173b770b386a9aed1e73f328f35ae6c', 'message': 'inductor: fix cpp_wrapper backward failing with CudaKernelParamCache assertion

When torch.compile(options={"cpp_wrapper": True}) is used with training,
backward graphs are compiled lazily after compile_fx() returns.  By that
time the config.patch({"cpp_wrapper": True}) context applied in compile_fx
has already exited, so compile_fx_backward skips get_cpp_wrapper_config()
and leaves store_cubin=False.  With store_cubin=False the cubin is never
written to CudaKernelParamCache, and DeferredTritonCallWrapper.generate()
then hits:

    AssertionError: CudaKernelParamCache not populated for
    triton_poi_fused_native_layer_norm_native_layer_norm_backward_1

Fix: apply get_cpp_wrapper_config() inside compile_fx_inner whenever the
cpp_wrapper kwarg is True.  This guarantees store_cubin=True and
autotune_at_compile_time are set for every cpp_wrapper compile regardless
of whether it runs eagerly (inside compile_fx's context) or lazily (after
it exits).  The patch is idempotent in the eager path.

Fixes https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/178845

Co-authored-by: Claude'}]

commits:
[{'sha': '08c125c1a7cddff062c590970afc742f61ccd5e3', 'message': "[c10d] Support stacked output tensors in Gloo all_gather_into_tensor (gh-178798)

ProcessGroupGloo::_allgather_base chunked the output tensor along dim 0
and required every chunk to match the input shape exactly. For stacked
outputs (world_size, *input.shape) the chunks retained the leading
dimension, e.g. (1, N) vs (N,), so the validation inside allgather()
rejected them. NCCL never had this restriction because it only validates
numel.

View the output tensor as the concatenated form before chunking so both
(world_size * N, ...) and (world_size, N, ...) layouts are accepted.
Use view() rather than reshape() to guarantee a zero-copy alias into the
caller's buffer.

Authored with Claude.

Fixes #178798"}]

outputs (world_size, *input.shape) the chunks retained the leading
dimension, e.g. (1, N) vs (N,), so the validation inside allgather()
rejected them. NCCL never had this restriction because it only validates
numel.

View the output tensor as the concatenated form before chunking so both
(world_size * N, ...) and (world_size, N, ...) layouts are accepted.
Use view() rather than reshape() to guarantee a zero-copy alias into the
caller's buffer.

Authored with Claude.

Fixes #178798"}]

[{'sha': '00604d0f7e9adbfb6a6ef06f375007945118044d', 'message': "[inductor] Fix FP8 dtype detection in _get_estimated_runtime

  _get_estimated_runtime() uses buf.node.maybe_get_dtype() to determine the
  dtype for peak TFLOPS lookup. For FP8 matmul ops (aten._scaled_mm), this
  returns the output dtype (FP16/BF16 accumulator precision), not the compute
  dtype (FP8). This causes compute-bound roofline times to be ~2x too high
  for FP8 kernels since FP8 has 2x the peak TFLOPS of FP16.

  Fix: inspect the IR node's input tensor dtypes. For _scaled_mm, both matrix
  inputs are FP8 while the output is FP16/BF16. If both inputs share the same
  dtype, use that as the compute dtype. This is generic and works for any
  future reduced-precision format (FP4, MX4, etc.).

  Also fixes two related issues:
  - device_info.py was missing torch.float8_e4m3fn (the primary NVIDIA FP8
    format used by _scaled_mm) and had duplicate e8m0fnu entries
  - get_device_tflops() Triton fallback would crash on FP8 dtypes due to an
    assert restricting to FP16/BF16/FP32; now falls back to FP16 TFLOPS"}]

[{'sha': '8c78a684fc120e6968c532373e6698315fe3d4b9', 'message': "Add parallel subprocess compilation and disk caching for NVGEMM backend

CuTeDSL's cute.compile() is not thread-safe (MLIR thread-local state), so
pre-compilation spawns one subprocess per kernel — the same pattern CUTLASS
uses with nvcc. A ThreadPoolExecutor in make_precompile_fn drives parallelism
across candidate kernels during autotuning.

Compiled kernels are exported as .so via export_to_c and cached on disk under
the inductor cache directory. On cache hit the .so is loaded via
cute.runtime.load_module (~1ms) instead of re-compiling (~100ms+ per kernel).

Authored with Claude.

[ghstack-poisoned]"}]

commits:
[{'sha': '4f8f8c787ab71234bcaedba1fc851e0ec461d5b3', 'message': '[profiler] Acquire GIL in PythonTracer destructor before Py_XDECREF

On-demand profiling via LibKinetoClient::stop() calls disableProfiler()
from a C++ thread without the GIL. This flows through finalizeTrace() →
getRecords() → python_tracer_.reset(), destroying PythonTracer on a
thread that never held the GIL. The Py_XDECREF(shared_ctx_) then crashes
in _PyObject_Free because pymalloc is not thread-safe.

Acquire the GIL before decrementing, and skip the decref entirely if the
interpreter is shutting down.

Authored with Claude.'}]

[{'sha': '40d8e1d5d718dc0506a22595e310223e949f52b7', 'message': 'Update on "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL LayerNorm kernel registration for SM100"


Register oink's CuTeDSL LayerNorm kernel as a CUDA dispatch override for `aten::native_layer_norm` via `torch._native`. On SM100 (Blackwell) devices with CuTeDSL installed, the oink kernel handles 2D bf16/fp16 inputs; otherwise falls back to the original CUDA kernel.                         
   
  - `layernorm.py`: Registration + gating logic. The adapter maps between aten's `(out, mean, rstd)` return order and oink's `(out, rstd, mean)` return order.                                                                                                                                        
  - `layernorm_kernels.py`: CuTeDSL SM100 LayerNorm forward (2-stage: mean pass then variance/normalization) and backward (separate dx and dweight/dbias kernels).
  - Tests added to `test_oink_kernels.py` for gating, registration, and correctness.

[{'sha': 'b9dbf83eb020ba8bc27e8761c63701a127696af7', 'message': 'Update on "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL softmax kernel registration for SM100"


Register oink's CuTeDSL softmax kernel as a CUDA dispatch override for `aten::_softmax.out` via `torch._native`. On SM100 (Blackwell) devices with CuTeDSL installed, the oink kernel handles 2D bf16/fp16 inputs on dim=-1 without half_to_float; otherwise falls back to the original CUDA kernel.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - `softmax.py`: Registration + gating logic. Additionally gates on `dim==-1` and `half_to_float=False`.                                                                                                                                                                                             
  - `softmax_kernels.py`: CuTeDSL SM100 softmax forward/backward/fused, with online softmax reduction and `torch.autograd.Function` wrapper.
  - Tests added to `test_oink_kernels.py` for gating, registration, and correctness.

[{'sha': 'b9dbf83eb020ba8bc27e8761c63701a127696af7', 'message': 'Update on "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL softmax kernel registration for SM100"


Register oink's CuTeDSL softmax kernel as a CUDA dispatch override for `aten::_softmax.out` via `torch._native`. On SM100 (Blackwell) devices with CuTeDSL installed, the oink kernel handles 2D bf16/fp16 inputs on dim=-1 without half_to_float; otherwise falls back to the original CUDA kernel.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - `softmax.py`: Registration + gating logic. Additionally gates on `dim==-1` and `half_to_float=False`.                                                                                                                                                                                             
  - `softmax_kernels.py`: CuTeDSL SM100 softmax forward/backward/fused, with online softmax reduction and `torch.autograd.Function` wrapper.
  - Tests added to `test_oink_kernels.py` for gating, registration, and correctness.

[{'sha': '7378ad1716a0c3f3742be00d3bd461885a3cb5dd', 'message': 'Update on "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL RMSNorm kernel registration for SM100"


Register oink's CuTeDSL RMSNorm kernel as a CUDA dispatch override for `aten::_fused_rms_norm` via `torch._native`. On SM100 (Blackwell) devices with CuTeDSL installed, the oink kernel handles 2D bf16/fp16 inputs; otherwise falls back to the original CUDA kernel via `call_boxed`.            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - `rmsnorm.py`: Registration + gating logic. Checks `cu.runtime_available()`, SM100 capability, input dtype/ndim before dispatching to oink.                                                                                                                                                        
  - `rmsnorm_kernels.py`: CuTeDSL SM100 RMSNorm forward/backward with stride-preserving pointer path and FP32 accumulation.
  - `rmsnorm_with_stage2_kernels.py`: Experimental cp.async ping-pong variant used as a fallback for specific N sizes (6144/8192).                                                                                                                                                                    
  - `test_oink_kernels.py`: Gating unit tests (no GPU needed), registration verification, and correctness tests (SM100+CuTeDSL required).

[{'sha': '57be60540352d647c0293a64324f6d420504678d', 'message': 'Update on "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL utility files for SM100 kernel registration"


Add shared utility modules under `torch/_native/ops/_oink_utils/` for oink CuTeDSL SM100 (Blackwell) kernels.                                                                                                                                                                                       
                                                                  
  - `lite_quack.py`: Lightweight local clone of the small subset of Quack helpers that the SM100
RMSNorm CuteDSL kernels depend on.
  - `fast_launch.py`: Host-side fast-launch helpers for CuTeDSL pointer entrypoints.                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  These are prerequisites for subsequent PRs that register oink rmsnorm, softmax, and layernorm kernels as aten op overrides via `torch._native`.

commits:
[{'sha': 'caf7a83c2d5b4c1f8af776398d4f040af77bb7b7', 'message': "[native] Add oink CuTeDSL RMSNorm kernel registration for SM100

Register oink's CuTeDSL RMSNorm kernel as an override for the
aten::_fused_rms_norm CUDA dispatch. On SM100 (Blackwell) devices with
CuTeDSL available, the oink kernel is used; otherwise falls back to the
original CUDA kernel.

Includes the main rmsnorm kernel, the experimental stage-2 variant
(used as a fallback path for specific N sizes), and gating/registration
logic following the torch._native framework patterns.

Tests cover gating function validation, registration verification, and
correctness against a reference implementation.

Authored with Claude."}]

[{'sha': '698db59198b8eb456064f7263cc8eb03dc4e1489', 'message': '[native] Add oink CuTeDSL utility files for SM100 kernel registration

Add shared utility modules used by oink CuTeDSL kernels (rmsnorm,
softmax, layernorm, cross_entropy) that will be registered via
torch._native in subsequent PRs.

- lite_quack.py: CuTe DSL base classes (ReductionBase, RMSNormBackward),
  row-reduction primitives, online softmax, SM100 cluster helpers,
  predicate/copy utilities, and atomic operations.

- fast_launch.py: Host-side optimization helpers for CuTeDSL pointer
  entrypoints that avoid per-call ctypes allocations for
  latency-sensitive kernel launches.

Authored with Claude.'}]

commits:
[{'sha': '698db59198b8eb456064f7263cc8eb03dc4e1489', 'message': '[native] Add oink CuTeDSL utility files for SM100 kernel registration

Add shared utility modules used by oink CuTeDSL kernels (rmsnorm,
softmax, layernorm, cross_entropy) that will be registered via
torch._native in subsequent PRs.

- lite_quack.py: CuTe DSL base classes (ReductionBase, RMSNormBackward),
  row-reduction primitives, online softmax, SM100 cluster helpers,
  predicate/copy utilities, and atomic operations.

- fast_launch.py: Host-side optimization helpers for CuTeDSL pointer
  entrypoints that avoid per-call ctypes allocations for
  latency-sensitive kernel launches.

Authored with Claude.'}]

The function handles both possible stack orderings: 1. `[..., NULL, locals_builtin]` — TOS is the builtin, NULL below it (returns 2) 2. `[..., locals_builtin]` — TOS is the builtin, no NULL (returns 1) 3. `[..., locals_builtin, NULL]` — TOS is NULL, builtin below (returns 2) This covers the Python 3.11+ calling convention where `PUSH_NULL` may appear either before or after the callee depending on how it was loaded. It generates bytecode to materialize the `locals()` result from the packed frame values that `compile_subgraph` places on the stack. **Stack layout at the point this code runs** (from `output_graph.py:2162-2169`): ``` [packed_frame_list], *(frame N stack items including call setup) ``` Where `packed_frame_list` is a list of lists: `[[frame N locals], [frame N-1 stack+locals], ...]` **Generated bytecode sequence:** 1. `POP_TOP` x `call_setup_items` — removes the `locals`/`vars` builtin (and NULL if present) from TOS 2. `create_copy(remaining_stack_items + 1)` — copies the `packed_frame_list` to TOS (it sits `remaining_stack_items + 1` positions deep after popping) 3. `LOAD_CONST 0` + `BINARY_SUBSCR` — extracts `packed_frame_list[0]` (frame N's locals) 4. This means the resume path never actually calls `locals()` — it gets a pre-built dict. This is semantically correct for zero-arg `locals()` at a graph break, since the frame state is fully known at compile time. 2. **Only frame N (innermost) is handled**: `all_stack_locals_metadata[0]` is always used. In the test case: ```python if x.dim() > 2: batch_size, seq_len, hidden_dim = x.shape x = x.view(-1, hidden_dim) x = x + 1 if "batch_size" in locals(): # ← snapshot taken here ``` At the point `locals()` is called, `batch_size` and `seq_len` are already in `tx.symbolic_locals` (assigned in the `if` branch), so they'll appear in the snapshot. #### Observations 1. **No more graph break for `locals()`/`vars()`**: This is a behavioral change — previously, zero-arg `locals()` and `vars()` always caused a graph break. Cell/free variable unwrapping (`builtin.py:1241-1242`) ```python if name in cell_and_freevars: value = tx.output.side_effects.load_cell(value) ``` This is a correctness fix. In `tx.symbolic_locals`, cell and free variables (captured by closures via `nonlocal` or outer-scope references) are stored as `CellVariable` wrappers, not as the underlying value. CPython's `locals()` returns the *contents* of the cell, not the cell object. Without `load_cell`, the snapshot would contain `CellVariable` objects where it should contain the actual values — which would break any code inspecting the dict. `load_cell` handles three cases correctly: - Pending mutation → returns the mutated value - Pre-existing contents → returns the original value - Uninitialized cell → triggers `unimplemented` (graph break), which is correct since CPython would raise `NameError` #### 2. commits:
[{'sha': '8b6e6564eccb1cacf7d01e0786442f97af1cec4c', 'message': "Defer pipeline RECV ops with rank-parity deadlock avoidance (#172668)

On some platforms (e.g., AMD ROCm), a pending RECV blocks unrelated
compute ops that share the communication fabric, creating pipeline
bubbles. This adds an `overlap_pp_comm` flag (default True) that, when
set to False, defers each RECV to immediately before the compute op
that consumes its data.

Deadlock is avoided via rank-parity P2P ordering: the lower-ranked side
(rank < peer) always flushes deferred RECVs before SENDs to that peer,
ensuring it posts RECV first and providing the matching target for the
higher-ranked side's SEND.

Made-with: Cursor"}]

commits:
[{'sha': 'b55b870e180a0e2ebb8a186cc75cad99121537de', 'message': '[fake tensor] Use torch.accelerator.is_available() in avoid_device_init

FakeTensorMode.avoid_device_init had a hardcoded chain of backend
checks (CUDA, XPU, HPU) that was missing PrivateUse1. This meant that
on systems with only a PrivateUse1 accelerator (e.g. TPU), tensor
constants created under fake tracing were unnecessarily forced through
CPU with a to_copy to device, instead of being created on-device
directly.

Replace the ad-hoc checks with torch.accelerator.is_available(), which
already handles CUDA, XPU, HPU, MTIA, and PrivateUse1 through the
unified accelerator API (at::getAccelerator in C++). This also
future-proofs the function against new accelerator backends.

cc zou3519 australopitek guangyey eellison

Authored with Claude.

[ghstack-poisoned]'}]

commits:
[{'sha': '84ae6d7ae2240f53a74c10cc749fc4f539577bd8', 'message': "[mergebot] Add explicit error when ghstack revert finds no revertable PRs (#178725)

`get_ghstack_dependent_prs` can return an empty list when the PR's
GitHub state is inconsistent (e.g. merged via ghstack push but still
showing as open). This caused `do_revert_prs` to crash with a cryptic
`IndexError: list index out of range` when accessing `shas_and_prs[0]`.

Add an explicit check with a clear error message before calling
`do_revert_prs`.

Authored with Claude."}]

commits:
[{'sha': 'c9aad74662db7305f31c3333c49f90b8a4a625a9', 'message': '[Triton][Inductor] Unify OUT_DTYPE code flow with ACC_TYPE and INDEX_DTYPE in Triton templates (#178795)

Summary:

Move `OUT_DTYPE` from a Jinja template variable (`{{OUT_DTYPE}}`) to a regular
Triton kernel option passed via `options_dict`, consistent with how `ACC_TYPE`
is already handled.

Previously, `OUT_DTYPE` was computed at each BMM/BADDBMM call site in `bmm.py`
and injected as a Jinja kwarg override. This duplicated the dtype-to-triton-string
conversion logic across multiple locations.

This diff:
- Changes `triton_bmm.py.jinja` to reference `OUT_DTYPE` as a kernel constant
  instead of a Jinja-rendered `{{OUT_DTYPE}}`
- Adds `OUT_DTYPE` to the `options_dict` in `MMTemplateConfigMixin._get_triton_configs`
- Introduces `_dtype_to_triton()` static method in `MMTemplateConfigMixin` to
  centralize the dtype conversion, used by both `_get_acc_type` and `_get_out_dtype`
- Removes the `out_dtype` guard in `tuned_bmm` that prevented Triton BMM templates
  from being used when `out_dtype` differs from the input dtype
- Adds `test_triton_bmm_out_dtype` test and updates `test_bmm_out_dtype` to
  validate the ATEN backend path separately

Fixes the revert in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/178287

Test Plan:
TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES=1 buck2 run fbcode//mode/opt-amd-gpu fbcode//caffe2/test/inductor:max_autotune_amd -- -r test_triton_bmm_out_dtype
TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES=1 buck2 run fbcode//mode/opt-amd-gpu fbcode//caffe2/test/inductor:max_autotune_amd -- -r test_bmm_out_dtype

Reviewed By: PaulZhang12

Differential Revision: D98484446'}]

commits:
[{'sha': '8be15b4c36ae16b7edf8dc692295ac189ff12b61', 'message': 'Fix xplat build: use PyObjectType::get() directly

Summary:
Forward-fix for D98670756 ([coor] Add _dtensor::mesh_get_process_group custom op).

The original diff added `{"PyObject", c10::TypeFactory::get<c10::PyObjectType>()}` to the
schema type parser's type map. On xplat builds, `TypeFactory` resolves to `DynamicTypeFactory`,
which calls `DynamicTypeTrait<T>::getBaseType()`. But `PyObjectType` is intentionally excluded
from `FORALL_DYNAMIC_TYPES` in `dynamic_type.h` (it's a Python concept, meaningless for
mobile/edge runtimes), so this causes 100 xplat build failures.

Fix: Use `c10::PyObjectType::get()` directly, which bypasses the DynamicTypeFactory path.
This is consistent with how the rest of the codebase handles PyObjectType (e.g., the
`isRegisteredOpaqueType` check in the same file uses `c10::PyObjectType::get()`).

Test Plan: xplat CI should now pass — the only failures on D98670756 were this build error.

Reviewed By: aorenste

Differential Revision: D98689012'}]

commits:
[{'sha': '55c6fa3640db27f87ff8a57ad7e7d7d29764259c', 'message': "[dynamo] Fix invoke_subgraph reuse breaking autograd graph under trace_autograd_ops

When `trace_autograd_ops=True`, Dynamo traces `torch.autograd.grad` into
the graph and relies on FakeTensor autograd reachability to determine which
gradients are None. `stamp_out_subgraph` (the invoke_subgraph cache-hit
path) created output FakeTensors via `torch.empty_strided(...,
requires_grad=True)` — leaf tensors with no `grad_fn`. This severed the
autograd graph: with 2+ invoke_subgraph calls of the same subgraph,
`autograd.grad` couldn't trace from the loss back to the original input,
returned `(None,)`, and the compiled function produced no output.

Fix: when `trace_autograd_ops` is active, establish a trivial autograd
connection between the stamped-out outputs and the requires-grad inputs
via a zero-valued addition, preserving the grad_fn chain needed for
reachability analysis.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

[ghstack-poisoned]"}]

commits:
[{'sha': '55c6fa3640db27f87ff8a57ad7e7d7d29764259c', 'message': "[dynamo] Fix invoke_subgraph reuse breaking autograd graph under trace_autograd_ops

When `trace_autograd_ops=True`, Dynamo traces `torch.autograd.grad` into
the graph and relies on FakeTensor autograd reachability to determine which
gradients are None. `stamp_out_subgraph` (the invoke_subgraph cache-hit
path) created output FakeTensors via `torch.empty_strided(...,
requires_grad=True)` — leaf tensors with no `grad_fn`. This severed the
autograd graph: with 2+ invoke_subgraph calls of the same subgraph,
`autograd.grad` couldn't trace from the loss back to the original input,
returned `(None,)`, and the compiled function produced no output.

Fix: when `trace_autograd_ops` is active, establish a trivial autograd
connection between the stamped-out outputs and the requires-grad inputs
via a zero-valued addition, preserving the grad_fn chain needed for
reachability analysis.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

[ghstack-poisoned]"}]

Previously this optional data pointer was not passed to dlDeviceToTorchDevice, which is not supported on XPU.
Now that is being passed along, the device can be correctly determined.

Fixes https://github.com/intel/torch-xpu-ops/issues/3077

Signed-off-by: Artur Kłoniecki <arturx.kloniecki@intel.com>'}]

This types are arguments in `_scaled_grouped_mm_v2`, `_scaled_mm_v2` and `_grouped_mm`. There should be an enormous amount of failures then - `optional` and `ArrayRef` (`IntArrayRef = ArrayRef` iirc) are common argument types... commits:
[{'sha': '223c27584ffd3be77e4986ec2e631544f383cad3', 'message': '[distributed] Fix case mismatch preventing default backend type for custom OOT backends

`Backend._plugins` stores keys in uppercase via `register_backend()`, but
`BackendConfig` normalizes values to lowercase via `Backend.__new__()`. The
comparison in `_new_process_group_helper` failed to account for this, so
`_set_default_backend(CUSTOM)` was never called for multi-backend configs
containing custom backends.

Additionally, when a Python ProcessGroup subclass replaces the original `pg`
object, the previously-set default backend was lost. Now the default backend
type is preserved on the replacement.

Fixes https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/178756'}]

['**![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat) Preserve 1D-weight semantics in MPS linear fallback** The new fallback assumes `weight` is always 2-D by calling `torch.mm(inp_2d, weight.t().contiguous())` and then reshaping with `weight.shape[0]`. `torch.nn.functional.linear` also supports 1-D `weight` (dot-product form), so for valid MPS inputs with `input.shape[-1] >= 256` this path now errors in `mm` (or would yield the wrong output shape), which is a functional regression for previously supported API usage. commits:
[{'sha': '3f18bd036386a563d1d60fa0536da1016ec00da6', 'message': 'Wire OcclTransport into ProcessGroupOCCL constructor

ProcessGroupOCCL now accepts a c10d::Store in its constructor and
creates an OcclTransport instance for TCP-based inter-rank
communication. The factory function forwards the store instead of
ignoring it.

Depends on: PR #177512 (OcclTransport)'}, {'sha': 'b66ab42fa46837c202e303918d91e5c35cff0784', 'message': 'Add tensorToHost and hostToTensor memory helpers to ProcessGroupOCCL

These helpers safely copy data between OpenReg device memory
(mprotect-guarded) and host buffers using the existing MemoryGuard
RAII pattern. tensorToHost copies device tensor bytes to a
std::vector<uint8_t>; hostToTensor copies back. Both require
contiguous tensors.'}, {'sha': '137d56cc9317809b335ad62f628cbd4b2f85225e', 'message': 'Implement send/recv data transfer in ProcessGroupOCCL

Wire up real point-to-point communication using OcclTransport.
send() copies device tensor to a host buffer via orMemcpy, then
transmits it over TCP with metadata in a wire header. recv()
receives into a host buffer and copies back to the device tensor.

Also fixes tensorToHost/hostToTensor to use orMemcpy instead of
MemoryGuard + std::memcpy, since orMemcpy handles its own
unprotect/reprotect cycle with proper copy-direction validation.

Adds test_occl_send_recv.'}]

Summary:
CONTEXT: D97799113 was reverted by D98681402. This relands the original change.

Port GitHub PR https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/172246 to internal. This adds composable_kernel (CK) backend
support for scaled-dot-product-attention varlen path on ROCm:

- Fix philox seed/offset access in varlen backward to guard on is_dropout
- Fix softmax_lse shape and stride for CK varlen forward path
- Add `is_ck_sdpa_available()` API to `torch.backends.cuda`
- Update varlen_attn fake to use correct logsumexp shape per backend
- Add CK backend parametrization to varlen attention tests

Co-authored-by: Chinmay Kuchinad ChinmayDattanand.Kuchinad@amd.com
Co-authored-by: Jeff Daily jeff.daily@amd.com


Test Plan: buck test on relevant test targets

Pulled By: haoyuz'}]

[{'sha': '95d9db83dcac3d3bba4c16e98271990f662c7a31', 'message': 'Fix incorrect comparison results for uint8 tensor vs signed scalar (#178716)

When comparing a uint8 tensor against a negative int8 scalar (e.g.
torch.le(uint8_tensor, int8(-19))), combine_categories() preserves the
tensor dtype (uint8), causing the scalar to wrap (int8(-19) → uint8(237))
and producing wrong results.

For comparison ops, re-promote via promoteTypes() when a wrapped-number
scalar has a different integral dtype than the computed common dtype.
This correctly yields int16 for uint8-vs-int8, keeping -19 as -19.

The check is type-only with zero runtime cost (no .item()/CUDA sync)
and only applies to comparison ops -- arithmetic promotion is unchanged.

Signed-off-by: saif <contact@saifmb.com>'}]
